title: What Research Says About Teens and Social Media
subtitle: 别再妖魔化社交媒体：青少年没你想的那么糟

paragraphs.en: As a parent of two boys at primary school, I worry about the issues associated with teenagers and social media. Newspapers are constantly filled with frightening accounts of drug addiction and aggressive behavior supposedly caused by violent videogames. But even when these accounts touch on real concerns, they do not really reflect the great mass of everyday teenage social behavior: the online chat, the texting, the surfing, and the emergence of a new teenage sphere that is conducted digitally.
paragraphs.zh: 作为两个小学男孩的家长，我对青少年与社交媒体相关的问题感到担忧。报纸上不断充斥着关于吸毒成瘾和据称由暴力电子游戏引发的攻击行为的骇人报道。但即使这些报道触及真实问题，它们也并未真正反映青少年日常社交行为的主体：在线聊天、发短信、上网浏览，以及一个以数字方式运作的全新青少年领域的出现。

paragraphs.en: New technologies always provoke generational panic, which usually has more to do with adult fears than with the lives of teenagers. In the 1930s, parents worried that radio was gaining an irresistible hold of their children. In the 80s, the great danger was the Sony Walkman. When you look at today's digital activity, the facts are much more positive than you might expect. Indeed, social scientists who study young people have found that their digital use can be inventive and even beneficial. This is true not just in terms of their social lives, but their education too.
paragraphs.zh: 新技术总会引发代际恐慌，而这种恐慌通常更多源于成年人的恐惧，而非青少年的真实生活。20世纪30年代，父母担心收音机会对孩子们产生不可抗拒的控制力。80年代，头号危险是索尼随身听。当你审视当今的数字活动时，事实比你预想的要积极得多。事实上，研究年轻人的社会科学家发现，他们对数字产品的使用可以是富有创造性的，甚至是有益的。这不仅体现在社交生活方面，也体现在教育方面。

paragraphs.en: So if you use a ton of social media, do you become unable, or unwilling, to engage in face-to-face contact? The evidence suggests not. Research by Amanda Lenhart of the Pew Research Center, a U.S. think tank, found that the most passionate texters are also the kids most likely to spend time with friends in person. One form of socializing doesn't replace the other. It expands it.
paragraphs.zh: 那么，如果你大量使用社交媒体，是否会变得无法或不愿意进行面对面接触？证据表明并非如此。美国智库皮尤研究中心的研究员阿曼达·伦哈特发现，最热衷于发短信的孩子也是最有可能花时间与朋友面对面相处的孩子。一种社交形式并不会取代另一种，而是对社交的扩展。

paragraphs.en: Kids still spend time face to face, Lenhart says. Indeed, as they get older and are given more freedom, they often ease up on social networking. Early on, the web is their third space, but by the late teens, it's replaced in reaction to greater independence. They have to be on Facebook, to know what's going on among friends and family, but they are ambivalent about it, says Rebecca Eynon, a research fellow at the Oxford Internet Institute, who has interviewed about 200 British teenagers over three years. As they gain experience with living online, they begin to adjust their behavior, struggling with new communication skills, as they do in the real world.
paragraphs.zh: 伦哈特说：“孩子们仍然会花时间面对面交流。”事实上，随着年龄增长并获得更多自由，他们往往会减少使用社交网络。早期网络是他们的“第三空间”，但到青春期后期，随着独立性的增强，网络的作用会被取代。牛津互联网研究所研究员丽贝卡·艾农在过去三年中采访了约200名英国青少年，她表示他们必须上Facebook了解亲友间的动态，但对此又怀有矛盾心理。随着在线生活经验的积累，他们开始调整自己的行为，努力掌握新的沟通技能，就像在现实世界中一样。

words.meaning: adj. 矛盾的，又爱又恨的

paragraphs.en: Parents are wrong to worry that kids don't care about privacy. In fact, they spend hours changing Facebook settings or using quick-delete sharing tools, such as Snapchat, to minimize their traces. Or they post a photograph on Instagram, have a pleasant conversation with friends and then delete it so that no traces remain.
paragraphs.zh: 父母担心孩子不在乎隐私是错误的。事实上，他们会花数小时更改Facebook设置，或使用Snapchat等阅后即焚分享工具来最大限度减少留下的痕迹。或者他们在Instagram上发布一张照片，与朋友愉快交谈后便将其删除，不留下任何痕迹。

words.meaning: v. 使最小化，尽量减少

paragraphs.en: This is not to say that kids always use good judgment. Like everyone else, they make mistakes — sometimes serious ones. But working out how to behave online is a new social skill. While there's plenty of drama and messiness online, it is not, for most teens, a cycle of non-stop abuse: a Pew study found only 15% of teens said someone had bullied them online in the last 12 months.
paragraphs.zh: 这并不是说孩子们总能做出正确判断。和其他人一样，他们也会犯错误——有时是严重的错误。但学习如何在网上行事是一项新的社交技能。尽管网上充满了戏剧性和混乱，但对大多数青少年来说，这并非无休止的欺凌循环：皮尤研究中心的一项研究发现，只有15%的青少年表示在过去12个月内曾在网上被人欺负。

paragraphs.en: But surely all this short-form writing is affecting literacy? Certainly, teachers worry. They say that kids use overly casual language and text-speak in writing, and don't have as much patience for long reading and complex arguments. Yet studies of first-year college papers suggest these anxieties may be partly based on misguided nostalgia. When Stanford University scholar Andrea Lunsford gathered data on the rates of errors in freshman composition papers going back to 1917, she found that they were virtually identical to today.
paragraphs.zh: 但所有这些短小精悍的写作难道不会影响读写能力吗？老师们确实为此担忧。他们说孩子们在写作中使用过于随意的语言和网络用语，对长篇阅读和复杂论证缺乏耐心。然而，对大学一年级论文的研究表明，这种焦虑可能部分源于错误的怀旧情绪。斯坦福大学学者安德烈娅·伦斯福德收集了1917年以来大一新生作文的错误率数据，发现它们与今天几乎完全相同。

paragraphs.en: But even as error rates stayed stable, student essays have blossomed in size and complexity. They are now six times longer and, unlike older 'what I did this summer' essays, they offer arguments supported by evidence. Why? Computers have vastly increased the ability of students to gather information, sample different points of view and write more fluidly.
paragraphs.zh: 然而，即使错误率保持稳定，学生论文的篇幅和复杂性却显著增长。如今论文长度是过去的六倍，且不同于过去“我今年夏天做了什么”式的文章，它们提供了有证据支持的论证。为什么？计算机极大地提高了学生收集信息、了解不同观点以及更流畅写作的能力。

paragraphs.en: When linguist Naomi Baron studied students' instant messaging, even there she found surprisingly rare usage of short forms such as 'u' for 'you', and as students got older, they began to write in more grammatical sentences. That is because they want to appear more adult, and they know how adults are expected to write. Clearly, teaching teens formal writing is still crucial, but texting probably isn't destroying their ability to learn it.
paragraphs.zh: 语言学家内奥米·巴伦在研究学生即时通讯时，甚至发现在那里使用“u”代替“you”等缩写形式也出奇地少见，而且随着学生年龄增长，他们开始写出更符合语法的句子。这是因为他们想显得更成熟，并且知道成年人应该如何写作。显然，教授青少年正式写作仍然至关重要，但发短信可能并未摧毁他们学习写作的能力。

words.meaning: adj. 语法的，符合语法规则的

paragraphs.en: It is probably true that fewer kids are heavy readers compared with two generations ago, when cheap paperbacks boosted rates of reading. But even back then, a minority of people — perhaps 20% — were lifelong heavy readers, and it was cable TV, not the internet, that struck a blow at that culture in the 1980s. Still, 15% or more of kids are found to be deeply bookish. In fact, the online world offers kids remarkable opportunities to become literate and creative because young people can now publish ideas not just to their friends, but to the world. And it turns out that when they write for strangers, their sense of authentic audience makes them work harder, push themselves further, and create powerful new communicative forms.
paragraphs.zh: 与两代人之前廉价平装书提升阅读率的时期相比，如今的重度阅读孩子确实可能变少了。但即便在那时，也只有少数人——大约20%——是终生的重度读者，而且对那种文化造成冲击的是20世纪80年代的有线电视，而非互联网。尽管如此，仍有15%或更多的孩子被发现极度热爱阅读。事实上，网络世界为孩子们提供了培养读写能力和创造力的绝佳机会，因为年轻人现在不仅可以向朋友发表想法，还可以向全世界发表想法。事实证明，当他们为陌生人写作时，那种“真实读者”的意识会让他们更加努力，不断突破自我，创造出强大的新型交流形式。

paragraphs.en: Few would deny that too much time online can be harmful. Some of the dangers are emotional: hurting someone from a distance is not the same as hurting them face to face. If we're lucky, the legal environment will change to make teenagers' online lives less likely to haunt them later on. Just last week, California passed a law allowing minors to demand that internet firms erase their digital past and the EU has considered similar legislation.
paragraphs.zh: 很少有人会否认过多上网可能有害。有些危险是情感层面的：远距离伤害某人与面对面伤害他们是不同的。如果我们幸运的话，法律环境将会改变，使青少年的网络生活不太可能在日后困扰他们。就在上周，加利福尼亚州通过了一项法律，允许未成年人要求互联网公司删除其数字历史，欧盟也在考虑类似立法。

paragraphs.en: Distraction is also a serious issue. When kids switch from chat to music to homework, they are indeed likely to have trouble doing each task well. And studies show that pupils don't fact-check information online — smart searching is a skill schools need to teach urgently. It's also true that too much social networking and game playing can cut into schoolwork and sleep. This is precisely why parents still need to set firm boundaries around it, as with any other distraction.
paragraphs.zh: 分心也是一个严重问题。当孩子们从聊天切换到音乐再切换到做作业时，他们确实可能难以做好每一项任务。研究表明，学生不会对网上信息进行事实核查——“智能搜索”是学校迫切需要教授的一项技能。过多的社交网络和游戏也会侵占学业和睡眠时间，这也是事实。正因如此，父母仍需为此设定明确界限，就像对待其他任何分心事物一样。

paragraphs.en: So what's the best way to cope? The same boring old advice that applies to everything in parenting: moderation. Rebecca Eynon argues that it's key to model good behavior. Parents who stare non-stop at their phones and don't read books are likely to breed kids who will do the same. As ever, we ought to be careful about our own behavior.
paragraphs.zh: 那么最好的应对方法是什么？就是那条适用于育儿方方面面、老生常谈的建议：适度。丽贝卡·艾农认为，关键在于以身作则。不停盯着手机、从不读书的父母很可能会培养出有同样行为的孩子。一如既往，我们应该留意自己的行为。
